food_subgroup: Rice and lentil porridge with vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general khichdi entry; no specific mix-vegetable khichdi variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated khichdi code. A0CKM (rice-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for khichdi or this specific dish variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Mix Veg Khichdi is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one medium bowl as typically served in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Khichdi (Rice and Dal preparation) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports plain khichdi (rice + moong dal). Values adapted upward for mixed vegetable addition: carbohydrate and fibre adjusted to reflect vegetable content (carrots, peas, beans, potato) at approximately 20% by weight. Protein and fat values retained from ICMR-NIN base. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. Sodium adjusted to reflect typical restaurant preparation with salt and ghee tempering.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice-lentil porridge / khichdi analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for mix veg khichdi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 based on analogous rice-lentil porridge entries (GI range 39–58 for moong dal khichdi and similar preparations). The combination of rice and moong dal lowers the overall GI compared to plain white rice (GI ~72) due to the protein and fibre contribution of lentils and vegetables. GL per serving calculated as: (50 × 18.5 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 27.75, rounded to 27.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salt and ghee tempering in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Mix Veg Khichdi contributes approximately 630 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice-lentil base with vegetable fibre moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 50 and a GL of 27.8 per 300 g serving, Mix Veg Khichdi produces a moderate glycemic response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a smaller portion (150–200 g) and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to further blunt postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high fibre from lentils and vegetables, and absence of trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mix Veg Khichdi is a heart-friendly dish with low saturated fat (1.2 g per 100 g), meaningful dietary fibre (1.8 g per 100 g), and no trans fat; it is suitable for cardiovascular disease management when prepared with minimal ghee and moderate salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mix Veg Khichdi is a traditional Indian one-pot comfort dish made by slow-cooking or pressure-cooking white rice and yellow moong dal (split mung beans) together with a medley of vegetables such as carrots, green peas, French beans, and potato, seasoned with turmeric, cumin, ginger, and a tempering of ghee or oil. The result is a soft, porridge-like preparation with a mild, earthy flavour and a smooth, slightly thick texture. Khichdi is one of India's oldest recorded foods, referenced in Ayurvedic texts as an easily digestible, balancing meal. In Singapore, it is served in Indian restaurants as a wholesome main course, often accompanied by papad, pickle, or yoghurt. Nutritionally, Mix Veg Khichdi provides a balanced macronutrient profile: moderate carbohydrate from rice, plant-based protein from moong dal, dietary fibre from both lentils and vegetables, and small amounts of fat from the ghee tempering. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low GI), making it more suitable for blood glucose management than plain white rice. It is naturally gluten-free, vegetarian, and halal. The dish is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its moderate glycemic load, for cardiovascular health due to its low saturated fat and fibre content, and requires sodium monitoring in hypertensive individuals when prepared with restaurant-level salt.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain khichdi data with adjustments for mixed vegetable content. No exact ICMR-NIN entry for mix veg khichdi exists; values are defensible estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice-lentil porridge entries. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients other than ghee; confirmed trace/negligible per ICMR-NIN plant food entries. Trans fat is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional khichdi preparation; confirmed zero by formulation. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input record appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded as it is not relevant to this dish. GI estimated at 50 based on analogous rice-lentil porridge entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for mix veg khichdi was found. GL per serving = (50 × 18.5 × 300) / 10000 = 27.75, rounded to 27.8.
